item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
poor Inaki :-( he tried to bring his friends back, and then realized that "make move" is not the same as "make live"… that he's lost loved ones and there's no way to undo that.

I find some of the complaints about the supplement silly. yeah, it's melodramatic; that's how people TALKED back then. and yeah, almost all of them were racist.

also, I love that Inaki loves Mr Roger's neighborhood and the joy of painting. that's really sweet. I like to think of him painting along with Bob Ross.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Source: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Gorilla_019.jpg
License: CC BY-SA 2.5

Title: File:Gorilla 019.jpg
Author: Kabir Bakie
Uploaded on: 2005

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know this is a delayed response but the intention was to express just how long this ape has been around. It was a loyal companion once, to someone part of a culture that has been otherwise erased (likely destroyed in their attempt to master life and death) - in a way, it is attempting to replicate that civilization, mimicking behavior it observed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The fact that the gorilla was able to make a functional stone-tipped spear breaks my immersion, not because of the anomalous intelligence necessary, but because scientists have attempted to teach flint knapping to real world apes (bonobos definitely, I don't know of any specific examples with gorillas), and found that the wrist anatomy of knuckle-walking apes, such as chimps and gorillas, precludes their making the kind of precise motions necessary to knap proper flint points. I would find a simpler tool, such as a stone hand axe, which can be made with rougher motions, more believable, since that's about the extent of what real world apes have been able to produce with training. Probably this won't bother anyone else, and it doesn't stop me from liking the article, for all the Michael Chrichton vibes it gives me, but I gotta point it out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You had me with the 'don't you die on me!' gorilla, lots of feels from that little guy. Totally cemented my love with the exploration log and the racist narrator (Phrenology, dude? Really?) meeting certain doom. All the +1's in the world for 2292's choice in programming.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it before, and after reading the related tale I think I understand it better. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry about missing those messages; kind of been in reclusive artist mode past couple months. ^~^;

I've been working more on that one though, and I think I'm almost ready to officially call it done- maybe tomorrow, if it still looks as good to me as it does as I type this (hard to call at the end of the day. X3)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nah. Completely unrelated. It was originally done, I *think* in order to justify some containment procedure that I later ended up getting rid of anyways.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had assumed it was a link to some other element in your mythos, and I was trying to work out which exactly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had debated that, about the pathogen - and think I will cut it. Was never really part of the original concept and I forget *why* I added it in the first place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked this one. It's a relatively fresh take on the tired talking animal trope and while it isn't breaking any new ground, it's doing a blackwood-style anomalous pulpy adventure story that also doesn't fall flat as so many others do by being too silly or humorous to the detriment of immersion.

You could probably condense or cut the material in the third to fifth paragraphs, as a lot of it isn't terribly relevant or interesting. The only really pertinent details are the pathogen(which is honestly the weakest part of the article, it's pretty bland and I think it could be cut entirely) and the discovery leading the exploration log. My feelings on the log itself are on its discussion page.

Overall, even though I feel it has some fat that could be trimmed, I like what you're doing here. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm really glad this worked out well. When I finish the MC&D file associated with this piece we ought to work out a crosslink or something. I would upvote, but as like half of this was my idea, I really don't feel comfortable doing so, nor do I feel that I deserve much credit, since you borrowed my idea and made it not suck lol.

+1 in spirit?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

For me this was a difficult sell until the logs. I loved the exploration logs! Very lovecraftian, very Heart-of-Darkness-y, very good tone in my opninion.

The interviews were also great… you took an otherwise lackluster idea (an immortal zombie-making gorilla) and made it interesting, real, and emotional. More proof that any idea can work well with the proper execution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with you of course, but that doesn't prevent me from calling the stupid man names! ;>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Of course Winchester was a fucking racist asshole. It wasn't that uncommon among the English (or, in fact, among whites in general) during the period, and it seemed from the rest of the group that Mr. Dark's primary criteria for this expedition were that you had to have a useful skill, and you had to be willing to do whatever had to be done to the locals to get the information he required. That's not likely to happen with, say, a band of abolitionists and missionaries…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Truth be told, the primary reason for the treatment has far less to do with actual ethics and more to do with gaining the cooperation of SCP-2292 and keeping it pacified. I'm going to just outright delete the physical analysis since, based on what I've seen, it seems fairly unnecessary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love gorillas.
I agree with TwistedGears and Jacob: the first collapsible only repeats what has been said. Perhaps keep it as 'show' and delete the 'tell', or the other way round?

I almost gave up and/or downvoted at the vivisection thing (which is why I advise you to keep 'tell' and discard 'show'… ;>), and especially at Harrison Winchester's journal (fucking racist asshole)… but the rest of the article quite made up for it!
I think the different aspects of the anomaly are a bit disjointed: disease by touch, plant-like heart, zombie-maker, sapiency, immortality, no sustenance required (why? how?)… I wonder if they are actually logically related, and how?
Yes, me too devoured by curiosity about 2292-3. And I totally love what you did with gorilla language!
+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm a little iffy on a few of the ploys here, but ultimately I still enjoyed it.

Your tone and writing style were generally solid, although the article seemed pretty long for what it was trying to say.

The supplement was a… strictly paint-by-numbers pulp adventure story, and while necessary to the backstory it didn't do much for me.

Also, I'm not particularly on-board with the idea that animal treatment ethics have advanced over time to the extent that they rule out invasive methods of necessary anomaly classification. I'm all for them recommending better animal treatment in general, but denying research methods only makes sense to me as a cost-benefit analysis. Here the vivisection probably wouldn't learn anything new, and communication and trust are now more important. If SCP-xxxx walks through the door shooting radiation of it every orifice tomorrow, they might have to cut that one open.

Ultimately I upvoted because the idea of a somewhat communicative gorilla being the last survivor of an ancient civilization is pretty cool, and I'm really curious why that civilization would need his particular abilities. It would've been nice to see a bit more hinted at there, but that's fine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very well-written and interesting read.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know the answer nyah nyah nyah nyah nyah

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks, I hope you check this again because I've been trying to message you about using that art you made related to 748 on my author page;p

Not a bad theory; I would say partly right. Though, SCP-2292 was not necessarily the greatest threat they had to deal with. But I'd like to keep some mystery there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
[Puts on expedition hat]

Well. This was a very entertaining article, with much that I enjoyed.

The first paragraph of the description was quite the wild ride and really caught my interest. From there discussion of Inaki's abilities was intriguing enough to maintain that interest. I do feel that the first collapsible is a little redundant, as most of the information contained there in is found in the description.

When I got to the addenda I was worried that the interviews may bog down what was to me a relatively fast paced article, however the development of Inaki's character and the mystery of Blue Flower helped give the article a solid finish.

Additionally, I found the supplement to be the best part of this article. It gives me a bit of a Lovecraft vibe and left me with the hope that you (or another writer) will go on to explore SCP-2292-3 further in a tale.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Shades of Dr. Moreau and Frankestein in a way that I really dig; you managed to find a very cool fresh take on some old tropes.

Enthusiastic +1.

Also, a theory
Which if correct makes this even more poignant...

SCP-2292's disease-inducing and resurrective effects were unintentional flaws in an experimental life extension treatment. Alternately, they may have been intentional but did not function as intended. Either way, both Blue Flower and their civilization failed to realize this until after widespread exposure to SCP-2292.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It isn't so much sad that it can't die. It doesn't understand quite why it remains and others keep going away. It knows that it is special but doesn't fully grasp the ramifications of that. Time, I imagine, is also perceived quite differently by it. It understands "before" and "now" but has no real time frame in which to anchor itself. It doesn't understand that it is immortal. All it knows is others go away but never itself. It is quite different from how we would view immortality. It doesn't understand it in a larger scale. It is, however, able to understand that what it reanimates aren't the same. That even when moving, there is a "change"; I'm trying to explore how it would come to view these things, within the limits of its intellect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm. Mixed feelings on this.

The collapsible of its physical analysis is pretty much pointless, first off. The writing is mostly good, what I feel might be small tone things here or there, but overall I feel like it doesn't amount to a whole lot. The immortal thing is sad it's immortal while everything else around it isn't. That's über-stock immortal story, and the bigger pull here is that it's an animal I guess? Props for making a talking animal that doesn't fall to the issues of so many of its brethren, but I feel like it doesn't rise beyond anything either.

Neutral voting for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yay, pulp-style gorilla civilization!

I love that the treatment of Inaki progressed to both more humane and more effective science along with that progression in the real world.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks to all who critiqued this! Vince deserves credit for the basic idea of this SCP (and intends to write a related tale(s); no rush!)
